referral_type.reason_must_be_provided_below: Elective new referral for initiation of Lemtrada therapy following inadequate response to prior disease-modifying treatments for relapsing multiple sclerosis. This is not a STAT request; patient is clinically stable without acute neurologic decline, but ongoing disease activity on MRI and recent relapses warrant escalation of therapy within the next 1–2 months as scheduling and insurance authorization allow.

patient_information.name: Emily Rose Patterson
patient_information.address: 7428 S Harvard Ave, Apt 304, Tulsa, OK 74136
patient_information.allergies: Allergic to: (1) Amoxicillin – developed generalized pruritic rash and hives at age 10, no respiratory involvement; (2) Sulfamethoxazole-trimethoprim – diffuse rash and facial swelling in college, treated in urgent care with steroids and antihistamines, advised to avoid all sulfa antibiotics; (3) Seasonal/environmental allergies to grass and ragweed pollen causing sneezing, nasal congestion, and itchy eyes. No known allergies to contrast dye, latex, or prior infusion medications. No history of anaphylaxis.

required_diagnosis__stat_reason.stat_reason: This is not a STAT/urgent request. Patient has relapsing multiple sclerosis (G35) with breakthrough disease activity despite prior therapy (2 relapses over the past 18 months and new T2 lesions on MRI). She is clinically stable at present without acute vision loss, new motor deficits, or rapidly progressive symptoms. Goal is escalation of disease-modifying therapy in a timely but non-emergent fashion, coordinated with insurance authorization and infusion center availability.

standing_lab_orders.lab_frequency: CBC and CMP to be drawn by infusion center prior to initiation, then monthly for 48 months after the last Lemtrada infusion, per REMS and treating neurologist protocol, with results faxed to Tulsa Neurology & MS Center (918-555-8035). Additional labs may be added by treating neurologist as clinically indicated.

notes__additional_comments.notes_additional_comments_line_1: Patient is a 39-year-old right-handed woman with relapsing multiple sclerosis diagnosed in 2016 after episodes of optic neuritis and sensory disturbances, confirmed by MRI brain/spine lesions and CSF oligoclonal bands. She has previously been treated with glatiramer acetate for 3 years and then dimethyl fumarate for the past 4 years but continues to have relapses approximately every 9–12 months, including a right leg weakness episode in May 2024 requiring a 3-day course of IV methylprednisolone. Most recent MRI brain (2024-09-30) shows several new T2 hyperintense lesions compared to 2022 imaging, without large enhancing lesions. Patient has no history of prior Lemtrada exposure. She understands the need for ongoing monthly labs and has reliable transportation and support from her spouse to attend appointments. She works part-time from home as a graphic designer and is able to schedule infusion days without major difficulty. No current pregnancy; last menstrual period 2025-11-25, and she is using a levonorgestrel IUD for contraception. She has been counseled on potential infusion reactions, risk of autoimmune thyroid disease, and infection risk, and has received updated vaccinations including influenza and COVID-19 boosters.
notes__additional_comments.notes_additional_comments_line_2: Recent screening labs (attached) show normal CBC and CMP, negative HIV 1/2 Ag/Ab screen, positive varicella-zoster IgG (immunity), and negative QuantiFERON-TB Gold test. No history of chronic hepatitis, HIV, TB exposure, or recurrent serious infections. Baseline blood pressure typically 110–120/70–78 mmHg; no history of cardiovascular disease or malignancy. Patient experiences mild baseline fatigue and intermittent paresthesias in her feet but is otherwise independent in activities of daily living and ambulates without assistive devices. Please contact office RN (Jordan Miles, RN) at (918) 555-8034 or via fax at (918) 555-8035 if there are any questions about premedication protocols (standard regimen requested: IV methylprednisolone, antihistamine, and antipyretic before each infusion), scheduling, or if any infusion reactions or abnormal lab results occur so that the treating neurologist can adjust the care plan promptly.
